coredata.dc:title: Detection and quantitative assessment of internal short circuits in lithium-ion battery packs based on differential voltage analysis and mahalanobis distance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Junkun
Jin, Li
Wang, Zhongao
Li, Quanhui
Yan, Xiaoxue
Lei, Ertao
Ma, Kai
Lyu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Accurate and prompt diagnosis of internal short circuits at an early stage is critical for preventing severe safety incidents and ensuring the reliability and safety of lithium-ion batteries. However, existing early-stage internal short-circuit diagnosis methods often rely heavily on high-precision battery models and large volumes of high-quality labeled training data, limiting their practicality and robustness in real-world applications. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a novel method for early detection and quantitative assessment of internal short circuits in lithium-ion battery packs, based on differential voltage (DV) analysis and Mahalanobis distance. The proposed approach extracts a median DV curve from the sorted terminal voltages of individual cells within a battery pack, which serves as a reference to characterize the normal cell behavior. The Mahalanobis distance between each cell's DV curve and the reference curve is then calculated and compared against a threshold to distinguish short-circuited cells from healthy ones. For the identified faulty cells, the short-circuit current and resistance are estimated by analyzing the differences between charging voltage curves across adjacent cycles, enabling precise quantification of fault severity. Experimental validation is conducted using simulated internal short circuits with varying severities. Results show that the proposed method can accurately detect short-circuited cells when the short-circuit resistance is less than or equal to 300 Ω. The maximum and minimum relative errors of short-circuit resistance estimation are 5.21 % and 1.20 %, respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Internal short circuit
Differential voltage
Mahalanobis distance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the remaining charge capacity and corresponding remaining charging time.
Early ISC detection based on variations in remaining charge capacity.
Remaining charging time induced by leakage due to early ISC at the end of two consecutive cycles.
Mahalanobis distances of each cell during cycle 1 without the occurrence of early ISCs.
Mahalanobis distances of individual cells during cycle 2 in the absence of early ISCs.
Mahalanobis distances of individual cells during cycle 3 with a short-circuit resistance of 300 Ω.
Mahalanobis distances of individual cells during cycle 5 with a short-circuit resistance of 200 Ω.
Mahalanobis distances of individual cells during cycle 7 with a short-circuit resistance of 100 Ω.
Mahalanobis distances of individual cells during cycle 9 with a short-circuit resistance of 50 Ω.
Mahalanobis distances of individual cells during cycle 11 with a short-circuit resistance of 10 Ω.
Mahalanobis distances of individual cells during cycle 13 with a short-circuit resistance of 5 Ω.

head.ce:title: Detection and quantitative assessment of internal short circuits in lithium-ion battery packs based on differential voltage analysis and mahalanobis distance

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No. 8, Shuijungang, Dongfeng East Road, Yuexiu District

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate and prompt diagnosis of internal short circuits at an early stage is critical for preventing severe safety incidents and ensuring the reliability and safety of lithium-ion batteries. However, existing early-stage internal short-circuit diagnosis methods often rely heavily on high-precision battery models and large volumes of high-quality labeled training data, limiting their practicality and robustness in real-world applications. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a novel method for early detection and quantitative assessment of internal short circuits in lithium-ion battery packs, based on differential voltage (DV) analysis and Mahalanobis distance. The proposed approach extracts a median DV curve from the sorted terminal voltages of individual cells within a battery pack, which serves as a reference to characterize the normal cell behavior. The Mahalanobis distance between each cell's DV curve and the reference curve is then calculated and compared against a threshold to distinguish short-circuited cells from healthy ones. For the identified faulty cells, the short-circuit current and resistance are estimated by analyzing the differences between charging voltage curves across adjacent cycles, enabling precise quantification of fault severity. Experimental validation is conducted using simulated internal short circuits with varying severities. Results show that the proposed method can accurately detect short-circuited cells when the short-circuit resistance is less than or equal to 300 Ω. The maximum and minimum relative errors of short-circuit resistance estimation are 5.21 % and 1.20 %, respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: As the primary power source for pure electric vehicles and a key component of intelligent microgrids, energy storage systems play a crucial role in energy storage, distribution, conversion, and supply. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used in these systems due to their high energy density, high output voltage, long cycle life, and lack of memory effect. However, when a large number of cells are assembled into battery packs, their electrical behavior, thermal response, and aging characteristics become increasingly complex [1–3]. In addition, with the continuous increase in specific energy and the growing complexity of application scenarios, safety issues caused by battery failures have become more serious. These challenges greatly affect the real-time monitoring and safety management of LIBs in energy storage systems. Therefore, developing effective fault diagnosis methods for LIBs is of great theoretical and practical importance to ensure their efficient and reliable operation and to improve the overall safety of energy storage systems [4,5]. Such efforts also support the reliable operation of intelligent microgrids and promote the large-scale adoption of pure electric vehicles.

From the perspective of accident causation analysis, internal short circuits (ISCs) are recognized as a major cause of thermal runaway in LIB systems [6–8]. Once an ISC occurs, it continuously generates Joule heat. If this heat is not effectively dissipated, it can cause a local temperature rise within the cell, accelerating internal side reactions and eventually leading to thermal runaway [9–11]. During thermal runaway, the chemical energy stored in the battery is released rapidly, which may result in combustion, explosion, and serious threats to human safety and property. Therefore, to improve the safety of LIBs in energy storage systems, early detection and warning of ISCs are essential [12,13].

Before progressing to thermal runaway, ISCs in LIBs typically undergo a gradual evolution process. Based on the resulting thermal and electrical responses, this process can be divided into three stages: early, middle, and late [14,15]. In the early stage, the short-circuit resistance is relatively high, leading to a slow voltage drop caused by continuous energy loss through the shorted path. The heat generated at this stage is minimal and can be effectively dissipated by the thermal management system [16,17]. During the middle stage, the short-circuit resistance decreases, accelerating energy loss and causing a more significant voltage drop. The thermal management system can no longer dissipate the generated heat efficiently, resulting in a noticeable temperature rise within the cell [18,19]. As the temperature continues to increase, the ISC worsens and progresses to the late stage. At this point, large-scale mechanical failure of the separator causes extensive internal shorting, a rapid voltage drop to zero, and the sudden release of stored chemical energy, leading to uncontrollable thermal runaway. This analysis underscores the importance of detecting ISCs at an early stage to prevent catastrophic safety incidents [20].

Existing methods for early ISC diagnosis in LIBs can be broadly classified into model-based approaches and machine learning-based approaches. For example, Feng et al. proposed an ISC detection method based on variations in parameters identified from an equivalent circuit model [21]. This approach transforms the detection problem into a model parameter estimation task, wherein the state of charge (SOC) and ohmic resistance are estimated online using measured cell voltage, temperature, and current data. The method detects ISCs by comparing characteristic parameters of the worst-performing cell against those of an “average” cell. Ouyang et al. developed an ISC detection method based on an average-difference model for battery packs [22]. A first-order RC equivalent circuit model incorporating ISCs was theoretically derived, revealing that ISCs lead to reductions in both open-circuit voltage (OCV) and ohmic resistance. They further introduced the concepts of parameter effects and dissipation effects associated with ISCs, and defined characteristic indicators such as OCV deviation, the rate of change of OCV deviation, and the variance of internal resistance differences. Detection is achieved by evaluating whether these indicators exceed predefined thresholds. Additionally, Feng et al. utilized data generated from a validated 3D electro-thermal coupled model to establish the relationship between ISC states and estimated parameters [23]. By tracking the consistency of model parameters, their method enables real-time monitoring of ISC evolution, regardless of the fault location. Currently, such consistency-based methods for detecting ISCs in series-connected battery packs have become relatively mature and are generally capable of identifying ISCs with resistance below 100 Ω within a finite time. However, when the short-circuit resistance is around 100 Ω, the resulting current is extremely small, requiring a prolonged duration for the algorithm to reliably detect the fault.

With the increasing volume and precision of battery cloud data, the development of machine learning-based methods for ISC detection has become feasible. Recent studies have successfully employed machine learning algorithms to detect ISCs and further investigate the relationships between ISCs and thermal runaway, as well as the triggering conditions of short circuits. Naha et al. [24] trained a random forest model using experimental data and validated it with ISC test results, demonstrating an accuracy of up to 97 %. Kriston et al. [25] applied principal component analysis and clustering algorithms to analyze 780 sets of simulation data, showing that clustering techniques can effectively distinguish different thermal runaway modes. Additionally, Li et al. [26] utilized machine learning methods, including random forests, support vector machines, and neural networks, to examine over 2500 sets of simulation data on mechanically induced ISCs, establishing safety boundaries under various mechanical abuse conditions. Qiao et al. proposed a detection method based on mean normalization and an unsupervised clustering algorithm [27]. This method first applies mean normalization to the terminal voltages among individual cells within a battery pack to amplify the difference in terminal voltage between short-circuited and normal cells, making the short-circuited cells easier to identify. Then, by inputting the normalized voltage after filtering, an unsupervised machine learning algorithm is used to achieve early ISC detection. Furthermore, Qiao et al. further developed an approach that uses characteristics such as the relaxation voltage of LIBs at different times as input and the short-circuit resistance as output [28]. With the help of machine learning algorithms like Gaussian process regression, they estimated the short-circuit resistance to achieve quantitative diagnosis of early ISCs.

In summary, existing studies have made notable progress in early-stage ISC detection for LIBs. However, several critical limitations remain:
(1)
For model-based methods, it is necessary to establish an equivalent circuit model or an electrochemical model for each individual cell within the battery pack. The accuracy of these models is highly dependent on their parameters, which means that the diagnostic results are limited by the model precision itself and are easily affected by parameter variations. More importantly, to meet the requirements of high voltage and large capacity, LIB packs in practical applications usually consist of a large number of cells connected in series and parallel. Therefore, building models for every single cell inevitably leads to enormous computational costs and burdens [29].
(2)
At present, machine learning-based ISC detection methods still mainly rely on experimental or simulation data as inputs, while algorithms developed directly using real operational data from automotive or energy storage batteries are relatively rare. As a result, it is difficult to evaluate the practical effectiveness of such methods. On one hand, most data collected during actual operation are unlabeled, and data with fault labels are scarce—especially those labeled with ISCs—making it difficult to develop machine learning algorithms based on such limited data [30]. On the other hand, as previously analyzed, early-stage ISCs are difficult to distinguish perfectly from other fault types based on signal characteristics. Therefore, machine learning algorithms are often used for generalized fault diagnosis, which primarily distinguishes faulty cells from normal ones, but struggles to achieve quantitative estimation of the ISC fault severity.
(3)
Model-based methods require substantial computational resources to maintain model accuracy, especially as the battery ages or operating conditions change, making it difficult to ensure real-time model maintenance and parameter updates. Although machine learning methods have strong capabilities in nonlinear feature extraction, their training and deployment typically demand high-performance computing resources, which are often impractical for embedded systems in electric vehicles or energy storage stations. The high computational resource requirements of both approaches limit their applicability in scenarios with stringent real-time demands and constrained hardware conditions—such as electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems—highlighting the urgent need to develop more lightweight diagnostic solutions.

To address the aforementioned challenges, this paper proposes a statistical analysis-based method for early ISC detection and quantitative assessment in energy storage LIB packs. Differential voltage (DV) curves for each cell and the median DV curve of the pack are extracted from the terminal voltage profiles. The median DV curve serves as a reference, representing the typical behavior of healthy cells. During fault detection, the Mahalanobis distance between each cell’s DV curve and the median reference is calculated. A cell is identified as experiencing an early-stage ISC if its distance exceeds a predefined threshold; otherwise, it is considered healthy. For the identified faulty cells, the short-circuit current and resistance are estimated based on the voltage profile differences between adjacent charging cycles, enabling a quantitative evaluation of fault severity. A series of ISC simulations with varying degrees of severity were conducted to validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed method. Compared to existing approaches, the proposed method offers significant advantages: it eliminates the need for battery modeling and associated parameter identification, thus avoiding the computational burden of model construction and updates. Furthermore, unlike machine learning-based approaches, this method requires no complex model training or large labeled datasets, offering improved practicality and applicability.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 elaborates on the proposed diagnostic methodology in detail. Section 3 presents the fault simulation process, followed by the diagnostic results and discussion in Section 4. Finally, Section 5 concludes the paper.

The proposed method comprises three main steps: (1) The DV curve analyzes the variation of voltage with capacity during the charging process, providing a sensitive reflection of internal parameter differences and state changes of the battery. In LIB fault diagnosis, the DV curve can amplify subtle voltage variations, thereby effectively identifying abnormal features caused by ISCs and offering a reliable basis for fault detection. Moreover, since the median is not affected by extreme values, it can effectively suppress the influence of outliers and abnormal fluctuations. Therefore, in ISC fault detection, this study extracts the median DV curve as a reference standard to represent the state of normal cells within the battery pack. Specifically, the terminal voltages of all cells within the battery pack are sorted at each time point to construct the median voltage curve. The corresponding median DV curve is then derived and used as a reference to represent the typical behavior of healthy cells; (2) The Mahalanobis distance between each cell’s DV curve and the median reference is computed. A cell is identified as experiencing an early-stage ISC if its Mahalanobis distance exceeds a predefined threshold; otherwise, it is classified as normal; (3) For cells identified as faulty in Step 2, the short-circuit current and resistance are estimated based on the differences in charging voltage curves between adjacent cycles, enabling a quantitative assessment of fault severity. The implementation involves three core components: extraction of DV curves, Mahalanobis distance calculation, and estimation of short-circuit resistance. The detailed procedures for each of these components are elaborated in the following sections.

As previously described, once the median terminal voltage among all cells within the LIB pack is obtained, the corresponding median DV curve must be extracted. It is important to reiterate that the term "median terminal voltage" refers to the statistical median of the terminal voltages of all individual cells within the pack. In addition, the DV curve for each individual cell is computed based on its terminal voltage. In this study, numerical differentiation is employed to derive the DV curves, with the calculation given by the following expression:
(1)
DV =
d V
d Q
≈
Δ V
Δ Q
≈
Δ V
Q
2
−
Q
1
where Q denotes the charging capacity of the cell, and V represents the terminal voltage under constant current charging conditions. Eq.(1) defines the DV based on an equal voltage interval ΔV, where Q 2-Q 1 represents the change in charging capacity within this interval. In this study, the DV is calculated using the equal voltage interval method. To adequately capture the features of the DV curves, ΔV is set to 1 mV. Both the median DV curve and the individual cell DV curves are derived using Eq.(1), implying an identical computational procedure. The only distinction lies in the input: the median terminal voltage is used for computing the median DV curve, whereas the terminal voltage of the corresponding cell is used for computing the individual cell's DV curve.

The DV curves obtained via the aforementioned numerical differentiation method are susceptible to measurement noise. Therefore, it is necessary to apply an appropriate filter to obtain smoothed DV curves. In this study, a moving average filter is employed for smoothing. The moving average filter smooths the signal by computing the average value within a predefined sliding window. Given a noise-contaminated time-varying signal s , the moving average filter can be formulated as follows:
(2)
s ˆ
r
=
1
2 ⋅
N
p
+ 1
⋅
∑
l = −
N
p
N
p
s
r − l
where s
r denotes the value of the original signal s at time step r, and
s ˆ
r
represents the corresponding filtered value. The window size of the moving average filter is 2 N
p + 1, where N
p is an integer. The parameter l indicates the time lag, and s
r−l corresponds to the value of the original signal at time step r-l. The literature [31] provides a detailed study on the window size of the sliding filter. With reference to this literature, the window size in this work is set to 31 to achieve a balance between information preservation and noise suppression.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Early ISC detection based on the Mahalanobis distance
ce:section.ce:para:
After extracting the median DV curve and the DV curves of individual cells, this study employs the Mahalanobis distance to quantify the similarity between each cell’s DV curve and the median DV curve, thereby detecting early ISC cells within the battery pack. Unlike the Euclidean distance, the Mahalanobis distance accounts not only for the distance between two points but also their covariance structure, rendering it more sensitive across varying distributions and scales. Specifically, the median DV curve and the DV curve of a given cell are denoted as D
med and D , respectively.
(3)
D
med
=
[
D
med
( 1 )
,
D
med
( 2 )
, … ,
D
med
( N )
]
(4)
D =
[
D
( 1 )
, D
( 2 )
, … , D
( N )
]
Where N denotes the length of the median DV curve and the individual cell’s DV curve. In multivariate applications, the Mahalanobis distance is preferred over the Euclidean distance because it is unitless and accounts for correlations among variables. Assuming the mean vector of both the median DV curve D
med and the individual cell’s DV curve D is μ , and their covariance matrix is Σ, the multivariate Mahalanobis distance of the sample vector D
med relative to μ is defined as:
(5)
d
M
(
D
med
)
=
(
D
med
− μ
)
⊤
∑
− 1
(
D
med
− μ
)

The Mahalanobis distance between D
med and D is defined as:
(6)
d
M
(
D
med
, D
)
=
(
D
med
− D
)
⊤
∑
− 1
(
D
med
− D
)

Based on the above procedure, the Mahalanobis distances for all individual cells within the battery pack can be computed. Cells exhibiting a Mahalanobis distance exceeding a predefined threshold are identified as having early ISCs, whereas those with distances below the threshold are classified as normal cells.

Upon detecting early ISC cells within the battery pack, this study further estimates their short-circuit current and resistance to quantitatively assess the severity and progression stage of the early ISC. The principles underlying the estimation of short-circuit current and resistance are described as follows.

For a given LIB type, its SOC–OCV curve is deterministic and invariant. Existing studies attribute the difference between the charging voltage curve and the OCV curve to electrode polarization. A charging voltage curve consistency hypothesis has been proposed and validated, stating that for cells of the same type with varying internal resistance, SOC, and capacity, their charging voltage curves can be superimposed through translation or scaling transformations.

In a series-connected battery pack, when two cells have different initial SOC, continuous charging delivers an equal amount of charge to both cells. As illustrated in Fig. 1, once Cell 2 reaches the upper voltage threshold at time t 0 and becomes fully charged, the charging process is terminated to prevent overcharging. At this point, the charge difference ΔQ between Cell 1 and its full-charge state represents the remaining charge capacity. By horizontally shifting the voltage curve of Cell 1 to align with that of Cell 2, the endpoint of the translated curve corresponds to t 0-Δt. Based on the charging voltage curve consistency hypothesis, if charging were to continue for Cell 1 until t 0+ Δt, both cells would reach full charge simultaneously. The time difference Δt thus represents the remaining charging time. Given the charging current profile, the remaining charge capacity can then be calculated from the remaining charging time.

Building on this concept, existing studies have proposed a method for diagnosing early ISCs. In cells experiencing such faults, additional charge loss occurs due to leakage through the ISC, as illustrated in Fig. 2. By comparing the remaining charge capacity of undercharged cells at the end of two discharge–charge cycles, one can determine whether an early ISC has occurred. The change in remaining charge capacity is computed from the difference in remaining charging time, which is then used to estimate the short-circuit current and resistance. However, this method requires that at least one cell in the pack be fully charged during each charging cycle. Under many dynamic operating conditions, this requirement can lead to prolonged detection intervals, preventing timely identification of early ISCs. Moreover, this approach determines the remaining charging time of the faulty cell by referencing the voltage curve of a normal cell—essentially relying on a horizontal comparison between the voltage profiles of the faulty and healthy cells. In other words, the early shorted cell is compared against a normal counterpart. Such horizontal comparisons are highly susceptible to inconsistencies among cells within the battery pack, potentially introducing significant errors in the calculation of the short-circuit resistance.

The method proposed in this study estimates the short-circuit current and resistance by analyzing the differences in charging voltage curves between adjacent cycles. Specifically, the latter charge–discharge cycle is denoted as Cycle 2, and the preceding one as Cycle 1. The charging voltage curve from Cycle 1 is compared with the voltage curve at the end of Cycle 2, as illustrated in Fig. 3. An interpolation is performed on the Cycle 1 voltage curve using the terminal voltage of Cycle 2, and the corresponding interpolated time is denoted as t 1. The end time of Cycle 1 is denoted as t 2. The time interval between t 1 and t 2 represents the remaining charging time, Δt. In other words, at the end of Cycle 2, an additional duration of Δt would be required to reach the same state as at the end of Cycle 1.

The remaining charge capacity Q Δ can be calculated using the estimated remaining charging time in conjunction with the corresponding charging current data.
(7)
Q
Δ
=
∫ t
t + Δ t
I ⋅ d t

The early ISC current I err is obtained using Eq.(8), where T c denotes the duration of one charge–discharge cycle.
(8)
I
err
=
Q
Δ
T
c

After the short-circuit current is determined, the short-circuit resistance is calculated using the average voltage U during the charging and discharging processes：
(9)
R
err
=
U
I
err

Once the short-circuit resistance and current are calculated, they can be directly used as quantitative indicators for assessing the severity of the short-circuit fault. A higher short-circuit current and lower short-circuit resistance indicate a more severe fault, corresponding to an increased risk of thermal runaway in the lithium-ion cell.

The method proposed herein differs from the approach described in the previous section in two key aspects:
(1)
Instead of defining the end of a charge–discharge cycle by full charge, the cycle is delineated by the period during which the SOC variation, calculated via the ampere-hour integral method, equals zero. This period serves as the diagnostic window for early ISC faults. Within this interval, normal cells exhibit no leakage current, ensuring accurate SOC calculation, and the voltage at the cycle’s end equals that at its start. Conversely, cells with early ISCs demonstrate a voltage drop at the cycle’s end relative to the beginning due to leakage current. This approach enables multiple detections across successive charge–discharge cycles, shortening the diagnostic interval and allowing fault identification even when full charge is not reached over extended operation, thus facilitating more frequent monitoring of early ISC evolution.
(2)
Rather than calculating the remaining charge capacity relative to full charge, the method uses the cell’s own voltage at the start of each charge–discharge cycle as the reference baseline. The remaining charging time is then determined by comparing the voltage at cycle end to this baseline, from which the remaining charge capacity is inferred. This capacity represents the leakage attributable to the ISC fault, from which the short-circuit current and resistance are subsequently derived. This approach does not rely on cell-to-cell consistency and therefore eliminates the need for horizontal translation or scaling of the charging voltage curves, simplifying the algorithm. More importantly, it mitigates the interference caused by intrinsic inconsistencies among cells within the battery pack, thereby improving the accuracy of short-circuit resistance estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fault simulation experiment and data acquisition
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the effectiveness of the ISC detection method, it is essential to accurately replicate the external characteristic responses of actual battery ISCs through rationally designed equivalent ISC experiments. The prevailing equivalent ISC experimental techniques can be categorized into five types: phase change material-based triggering, shape memory alloy-based triggering, dendrite-induced triggering, mechanical triggering, and external resistor triggering. Comprehensive comparisons reveal that the external resistor triggering method not only offers excellent experimental repeatability but also enables precise control of the ISC resistance, making it particularly suitable for parameter calibration and validation of ISC detection models. Given its superior controllability and reproducibility, all equivalent ISC experiments in this study were systematically designed and conducted using the external resistor triggering approach.

The experimental platform employs a closed-loop measurement and control architecture within a high-precision constant-temperature environment, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The testing system operates inside a precision thermostatic chamber maintained at 25°C to eliminate temperature fluctuations that could interfere with electrochemical measurements. At its core, the system comprises a high-accuracy battery testing unit, with charge-discharge protocols programmatically executed via a host computer. A communication protocol establishes a real-time data acquisition channel at a 1 Hz sampling frequency, synchronously monitoring multidimensional parameters including individual cell terminal voltage, current, and surface temperature. The battery pack used in the experiments consists of eight 21700 cylindrical LIB cells connected in series, with the specifications of the individual cells provided in Table 1. By integrating a multiphysics synchronous data acquisition system with closed-loop feedback control, the platform achieves programmable test execution and precise timing in data collection, thereby ensuring high reliability and reproducibility of experimental data. The system architecture is meticulously designed to meet the stringent demands of electrochemical testing, providing a robust hardware foundation for subsequent experimental investigations.

This study employed a constant current–dynamic stress test (CC-DST) cycling protocol to systematically evaluate the series-connected battery pack. The specific experimental procedure was as follows: during the charging phase, the pack was charged at a constant current of 0.5 C and the charging process was immediately terminated once any individual cell reached 4.2 V (charge cutoff voltage) to prevent overcharging. The discharge phase utilized a DST mode to emulate dynamic load variations under realistic operating conditions. Discharge was halted when any cell voltage dropped to 2.75 V (discharge cutoff voltage) to avoid over-discharge. This charge–discharge cycle was repeated 14 times to assess the performance evolution of the battery pack over successive cycles. To investigate the progression of early ISC, different resistors (listed in Table 2) were paralleled across cells 4 and 8, with their connection controlled via manual switches. By toggling various switch combinations, ISCs of varying severities were simulated: lower short-circuit resistance corresponded to higher short-circuit current and thus more severe faults. Different short-circuit resistors were introduced sequentially at each cycle stage to mimic the gradual deterioration of ISC from mild to severe. This experimental design allows precise control over the timing and severity of ISC induction, providing a reliable method to study the electrothermal behavior evolution of battery packs under early ISC conditions.

Next, the Mahalanobis distance between the DV curve of each cell and the median DV curve was calculated and compared against a predetermined threshold to detect early ISCs. Based on the experimental analysis results, for the battery pack used in the experiment, we found that during the first two charge-discharge cycles when no ISC fault occurred within the battery pack, the maximum Mahalanobis distance among all individual cells was 0.097. In other words, during normal operation of the battery pack, the maximum Mahalanobis distance of the cells was 0.097. Therefore, based on this result and to allow a certain margin to prevent misdiagnosis, we set the ISC detection threshold to a value slightly larger than 0.097, specifically 0.10. It should be noted that in practical applications, this threshold can be flexibly adjusted according to battery type, usage scenarios, and other factors to enhance the sensitivity of the fault detection algorithm. The Mahalanobis distances of individual cells across different charge-discharge cycles are presented in
Figs. 5 through 12, where the dashed lines indicate the threshold. From Figs. 5–12, it can be observed that during the first two cycles, the Mahalanobis distances of all cells remained below the threshold, indicating that all cells were in a normal state without early ISCs during these cycles.

Starting from the third cycle, the Mahalanobis distances of Cell 4 and Cell 8 exceeded the threshold, whereas those of the other cells stayed below it. This suggests the onset of early ISCs in Cells 4 and 8 from the third cycle onward, while the remaining cells remained healthy. Moreover, it is evident that as the short-circuit resistance decreased, the Mahalanobis distances for Cells 4 and 8 increased correspondingly. This trend indicates that as the early ISCs worsened, the Mahalanobis distance of the affected cells progressively grew. The diagnostic results align well with the previously described fault injection scenarios, demonstrating that the proposed method can accurately identify early ISC cells within the LIB pack, thereby validating its effectiveness. Similar conclusions can be drawn from the fault detection results obtained at the 4th, 6th, 8th, 10th, 12th, and 14th charge-discharge cycles; for brevity, these are not discussed further herein.

After detecting the early ISC cells within the battery pack, the previously proposed method was further employed to estimate their short-circuit resistances, thereby quantifying the severity and evolution stage of the early ISCs. The true values, estimated values, and relative errors of the short-circuit resistances for cells 4 and 8 are summarized in Table 3. As shown in Table 3, the maximum relative error of the short-circuit resistance estimation for cell 4 is 5.21 %, while that for cell 8 is 4.89 %. The estimation errors remain within an acceptable range, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed quantitative evaluation method for early ISCs.

As previously mentioned, as the short-circuit resistance decreases, the Mahalanobis distance corresponding to the ISC cell increases significantly, while the estimation error of the short-circuit resistance decreases. This phenomenon occurs because the reduction in short-circuit resistance causes a sharp increase in the current through the ISC pathway, leading to strong disturbances in the transport of electrons and lithium ions in the local region. Consequently, more intense charge redistribution and localized polarization occur between the positive and negative electrodes. Specifically, a lower short-circuit resistance allows a large amount of current to bypass the normal external circuit and close the loop directly inside the cell, resulting in a substantially higher effective discharge rate, increased polarization overpotential, and a rapid drop in terminal voltage. Meanwhile, the high current density in the short-circuit region induces significant Joule heating, accelerating local temperature rise and side reactions (such as solid electrolyte interphase film decomposition and lithium dendrite growth), which further aggravate active material loss and irreversible electrode degradation. These microscopic processes collectively lead to increasingly pronounced and abrupt voltage drops caused by ISCs as the short-circuit resistance decreases.

A more pronounced voltage drop implies that the abnormal variations in the DV curve become more significant, ultimately leading to a gradual increase in the Mahalanobis distance corresponding to the ISC cell. In addition, the increasingly evident voltage drop enables the proposed short-circuit resistance estimation method to better capture the ISC fault characteristics, which ultimately manifests as a gradual reduction in the short-circuit resistance estimation error.

It should be noted that the method proposed in this paper requires obtaining the charging voltage curve during the constant-current charging phase, which means it cannot operate online in real time. However, the method is fundamentally based on statistical analysis. Its implementation does not require building a battery model, nor does it need large amounts of training data and substantial computational resources like machine learning methods. In other words, the method presented here requires only minimal computational resources to achieve accurate diagnosis and quantitative assessment of ISC. Therefore, the ISC diagnostic method proposed in this paper can be practically deployed in real-world scenarios such as electric vehicles and grid energy storage systems, demonstrating strong practical applicability.

To further highlight the advantages of the proposed method, this study selects representative approaches from existing research as benchmarks and conducts both qualitative and quantitative comparisons with them. First, we conducted a qualitative comparison between the baseline methods and the proposed method based on four criteria: whether a battery model is required, whether an offline training process is needed, whether labeled data is necessary, and whether quantitative estimation of short-circuit resistance can be achieved. The comparison results are summarized in Table 4.

As shown in Table 4, compared to existing methods, the proposed approach demonstrates several key advantages. Firstly, it eliminates the need for battery modeling, thereby avoiding the computational burden associated with model construction and parameter updates. Secondly, the method requires no training process, which circumvents complex computations and time-consuming training phases, making it easier to deploy and implement in practical battery management systems. Thirdly, as the method is fundamentally based on statistical analysis, it operates without requiring labeled data—this aligns well with real-world scenarios where obtaining labeled fault data for training is challenging. Finally, the proposed method achieves quantitative estimation of short-circuit resistance, enabling battery management systems to accurately assess the severity of ISC faults and implement targeted countermeasures accordingly.

As observed, among the three baseline methods in Table 4, only the DEKF-based approach achieves quantitative estimation of short-circuit resistance. To further highlight the advantages of the proposed method, we compared its short-circuit resistance estimation results with those of the DEKF method. Specifically, taking Cell 4 with an ISC as an example, the relative errors of the estimated short-circuit resistance obtained by the two methods are summarized in Table 5. The results in Table 5 demonstrate that, under different true short-circuit resistance values, the estimation errors of the proposed method are consistently smaller than those of the baseline method, further validating the superiority of our approach. The same conclusion can be drawn from the short-circuit resistance estimation results of Cell 8 with an ISC. For brevity, these results are not discussed in detail here.

This paper proposes a statistical analysis-based method for early ISC detection and quantitative estimation in LIB packs. By leveraging DV curves and Mahalanobis distance, the method detects and distinguishes normal cells from internally shorted cells within the battery pack. For the identified shorted cells, the short-circuit current and resistance are accurately estimated based on the differences in charging voltage curves between adjacent cycles, enabling quantitative diagnosis of ISC faults. Experimental results under varying severity levels of ISCs validate the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed approach. The main conclusions are as follows:
(1)
The method achieves ISC diagnosis without relying on an explicit battery model, thereby avoiding the challenging issue of constructing precise battery models and the high computational complexity associated with model parameter updates;
(2)
It eliminates the need for complex model training and large volumes of labeled data, addressing the practical scenario where fault-labeled data—especially for ISCs—are scarce during actual battery operation;
(3)
As detailed in Section 2, the computational formulas for DV extraction, Mahalanobis distance calculation, and short-circuit resistance estimation are straightforward and intuitive, requiring minimal computational resources. This facilitates easy deployment and embedding within onboard or stationary energy storage battery management systems, enhancing practical applicability;
(4)
Beyond early fault detection, the method also quantifies the short-circuit current and resistance of faulty cells, supporting battery management systems in accurately assessing fault severity and implementing targeted mitigation strategies. Additionally, the sequential detection and quantitative evaluation framework enhances efficiency by focusing resistance estimation only on detected faults, thereby avoiding unnecessary computations.
(5)
Experimental results under different severity levels of ISC fault scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method can accurately identify the faulty cell within the battery pack when the short-circuit resistance is 300 Ω or less. For the detected faulty cells, the maximum relative error in estimating the short-circuit resistance is 5.21 %, while the minimum relative error is only 1.20 %.

Future research efforts can be carried out in the following aspects: Firstly, in practical BMS, the presence of balancing systems affects the short-circuit resistance estimation process in the proposed method. Therefore, one of the primary tasks in future research will be to accurately estimate short-circuit resistance under conditions where balancing systems are present. Secondly, future research could focus on developing adaptive threshold adjustment methods to accommodate changes in operational conditions such as temperature and state of health, thereby further enhancing the adaptability of the ISC detection method. Finally, extreme operating conditions, battery aging, and multiple fault scenarios may pose potential interference to the ISC diagnostic method proposed in this paper. Therefore, future work will focus on investigating the impact of extreme operating conditions on the proposed method, while further refining it to ensure applicability in scenarios involving battery aging and multiple faults.

Kai Ma: Project administration. Ertao Lei: Project administration. Xiaoxue Yan: Funding acquisition. Quanhui Li: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Zhongao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Conceptualization. Li Jin: Visualization, Project administration, Data curation. Junkun Zhang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chao Lyu: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research in this study received financial support from the scientific and technological projects of China Southern Power Grid Co., Ltd. Specifically, it was funded under the project number GDKJXM20230817.
